summary: This notebook demonstrates a complete feature engineering and inference pipeline for a polymer property prediction task. It augments the training set with external datasets by merging on canonicalized SMILES, then computes a rich set of molecular descriptors, graph topology metrics, and transformer-based embeddings clustered via KMeans and HDBSCAN. The processed features are saved and subsequently used to load pre-trained AutoGluon models for each target variable. A notable implementation detail involves directly copying known training labels to the test set where SMILES overlap exists before generating the final submission.
competition_flow: Loads raw CSV and external Excel data, augments targets, computes molecular/graph/embedding features, saves processed datasets, loads pre-trained AutoGluon predictors per target to generate test predictions, and exports a submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads train/test CSVs and external Excel files using pandas
Canonicalizes SMILES strings via RDKit to prevent duplicates
Loads pre-saved features and models via pickle and AutoGluon's load method
data_processing: Canonicalizes SMILES to standardize molecular representations
Augments training targets by merging external datasets and imputing missing values
Replaces infinities with NaNs and imputes missing test features with training means
Clips test feature values to training min/max bounds
Applies log10 transformation to the Ipc feature
features_engineering: RDKit descriptors (e.g., BCUT2D, Chi indices, SlogP, VSA, substructure counts)
Graph topology features (diameter, average shortest path, max degree, closeness/betweenness/eigenvector/Katz centrality statistics, ring count/size, heteroatom ratio)
ChemBERTa and MolFormer embedding clusters via KMeans (20 clusters) and HDBSCAN (two parameter configurations)
insights: External data merging on SMILES effectively expands training coverage for missing targets
Transformer embeddings paired with clustering yield meaningful structural groupings for tabular learning
Clipping and mean-imputation stabilize test predictions against distribution shifts
Direct label copying on overlapping SMILES guarantees perfect performance on those samples
critical_findings: The notebook explicitly copies training target values to the test set for any overlapping SMILES, which is a direct data leakage technique.
External datasets are merged directly on SMILES without validation, potentially introducing label noise or distribution shifts.
